html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Thirty-Two — Complete

The Aramaic Apocalypse

4Q246

A Qumran Cave 4 Aramaic Fragment — DSS Attested

Columns 1 and 2

He Shall Be Called Son of the Most High — Until the People of the Creator Arise — His Kingdom Is an Everlasting Kingdom — The Sword Shall Cease From the Land — He Shall Judge the Earth in Righteousness

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls — Cave 4 Qumran — 4Q246 — Aramaic — Dated 25 BCE to 50 CE

DSS Primary and Sole Authority — Independent Aramaic Apocalyptic Text

Parallel Tradition to Daniyel 7 — Predates the New Testament by at Least One Generation

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

4Q246 — THE ARAMAIC APOCALYPSE — COLUMNS 1 AND 2

EDITORIAL NOTE — EDITORIAL NOTE ON TEXTUAL INTERPRETATION — 4Q246 is a fragmentary Aramaic text found in Cave 4 at Qumran, dated to approximately 25 BCE through 50 CE — predating the New Testament by at least one generation. The phrases Son of God and Son of the Most High in Column 1 were later appropriated verbatim by the New Testament authors (Luke 1:32 and 1:35). Christianity borrowed this language from this pre-existing Aramaic tradition — it did not originate it. The interpretation of this fragment is genuinely contested among scholars. Column 1 describes a figure who claims divine titles and whose reign brings tribulation and trampling — most likely a foreign oppressor king using blasphemous royal honorifics. Column 2 begins with the arising of the people of the Creator and the establishment of an everlasting kingdom — but the subject of Column 2 is not clearly identified because the beginning of the document is lost. Two honest readings exist: (1) Column 2 continues describing the foreign oppressor, whose power is temporarily granted by Creator as an instrument of judgment before the covenant people are finally vindicated — consistent with how YHWH used Nevukhadnetzar in Daniyel. (2) Column 2 shifts subject entirely at the arising of the covenant people — and from that point the Creator's help, the everlasting kingdom, and the peace all belong to the covenant people and their leader, not to the imposter of Column 1. This reading mirrors Daniyel 7 precisely. Both readings are presented in the critical notes. The reader is encouraged to weigh the evidence. This text contains no Christian theology, no New Testament influence, and no Messianic framework as defined by Christianity or Rabbinic tradition. It is preserved here solely on the basis of its Dead Sea Scrolls attestation as an authentic Qumran Aramaic document.

4Q246 — COLUMN 1 A Vision Is Brought Before the King — Tribulation Shall Come Upon the Earth — Kingdoms Rise and Fall — He Shall Be Called Son of the Most High — Until the People of the Creator Arise

1 A vision was brought before him and he fell before the throne.

2 Then he arose and said to the king: O king, your anger shall subside, and be not terrified at the vision.

3 Affliction shall come upon the earth — great slaughter in the provinces.

4 The king of Assyria and of Egypt — he shall be great over the earth.

5 And all shall serve him, and all shall obey him.

6 He shall be called great, and by his name shall he be designated.

7 He shall be named the son of the great Creator, and by his name shall he be hailed.

8 He shall be called the Son of the Most High, and like the appearance of comets so shall be his kingdom.

9 For years he shall reign over the earth and shall trample everything underfoot.

10 People shall trample people and province shall trample province — until the people of the Creator arise.

11 And all shall rest from the sword.

12 His kingdom shall be an everlasting kingdom, and all his ways in truth. He shall judge the earth in righteousness.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4Q246 was discovered in Cave 4 at Qumran in 1958 and first published by Joseph Fitzmyer in 1974. Written in Aramaic in a Herodian-period script dated to approximately 25 BCE through 50 CE — predating the New Testament. Two columns survive. The beginning of the document is lost, which is the primary reason the identity of the subjects in both columns remains disputed. The Cave 4 provenance and Aramaic scribal hand confirm it as a Qumran covenant community text in the same tradition as the Aramaic Chanokh manuscripts and the Daniyel fragments. It is catalogued as 4Q246 in the official Dead Sea Scrolls register.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez tradition does not preserve 4Q246. The text is known exclusively through the Qumran Aramaic manuscript. Column 1's portrait of a foreign ruler who claims divine titles and brings tribulation upon the covenant people is consistent with the covenant prophetic framework preserved throughout the Orit Ge'ez prophetic books — particularly Yesha'yahu 14, Yehezkel 28, and the Daniyel material — where foreign kings who claim divine status are ultimately brought down by YHWH. The titles in verses 7-8 place the figure in the category of the covenant adversary, not the covenant leader.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic tradition does not preserve 4Q246. The language of verses 7-8 — Son of the great Creator and Son of the Most High — has no parallel in the Masoretic canon except through the Daniyel 7 tradition. The trajectory of Column 1 follows the covenant prophetic pattern of the arrogant foreign king: he rises, claims divine status, tramples the covenant people, and then — in verse 10 — the covenant people arise and the sword rests. This structural pattern recurs throughout the covenant prophetic literature from Yesha'yahu through Daniyel.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verses 7-8 — he shall be called the son of the great Creator and he shall be called the Son of the Most High. The critical question is whether these titles are the blasphemous self-designation of the foreign oppressor of verses 3-9 — the most natural reading given the trampling and tribulation that follows — or whether the text has already shifted at this point to describe a covenant figure whose divine appointment is being announced before the tribulation begins. The loss of the document's opening makes certainty impossible. What is clear is that verse 10 — until the people of the Creator arise — marks a decisive turning point in the text, after which the subject and tone shift entirely in Column 2.

4Q246 — COLUMN 2 The People of the Creator Arise — His Kingdom Is an Everlasting Kingdom — The Creator Is His Strength — All Shall Make Peace — The Sword Shall Cease From the Land — He Shall Judge the Earth in Righteousness

1 Until the people of the Creator arise, and all shall rest from the sword.

2 His kingdom is an everlasting kingdom, and all his paths in truth. He shall judge the earth in righteousness.

3 And all peoples shall make peace, and all shall serve him.

4 The great Creator shall be his strength, and he shall make war on his behalf.

5 He shall give the peoples into his hand, and cast them all down before him.

6 His sovereignty shall be an everlasting sovereignty, and all the boundaries of the earth shall be his.

7 The sword shall not be lifted against the people, and no people shall take up arms against them.

8 The great Creator shall be with him — he shall give them into his hand.

9 And they shall all be subject before him — the great Creator is his strength.

10 He shall wage war on his behalf and shall give the peoples into his hand — kings and governors shall prostrate themselves before him.

11 All the peoples shall serve him and the great Creator shall be his strength and his power.

12 He shall reign over the whole earth in righteousness and all shall make peace — the sword shall cease from the land.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Column 2 is the better-preserved of the two surviving columns and contains the theological climax of the fragment. The Aramaic phrase am El — people of the Creator — in verse 1 is the structural turning point of the entire text. The DSS manuscript makes no editorial break between Column 1 and Column 2 — the transition from tribulation to the everlasting kingdom is continuous, which is why the subject of Column 2 remains disputed. The Qumran community who copied and preserved this fragment understood themselves as the people of the Creator whose arising and vindication the text describes.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez tradition does not preserve 4Q246. The vision of eternal peace in verse 7 — the sword shall not be lifted against the people — resonates directly with Yesha'yahu 2:4 and Mikhah 4:3, both preserved in full in the Orit Ge'ez canon: they shall beat their swords into plowshares and nation shall not lift sword against nation. The convergence of sword-ceasing imagery across the Afro-Asiatic covenant prophetic literature and this Qumran Aramaic fragment places 4Q246 within the same ancient covenant eschatological vision that the Orit Ge'ez preserves independently.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic tradition does not preserve 4Q246. Verse 2 — his kingdom is an everlasting kingdom and all his paths in truth, he shall judge the earth in righteousness — is directly parallel to Daniyel 7:14 and 7:27. The parallel establishes that both texts draw on the same Aramaic apocalyptic covenant tradition. In Daniyel 7:27 the everlasting kingdom and the judgment belong explicitly to the people of the Most High — not to an individual ruler. The 4Q246 parallel am El — people of the Creator — in Column 2 verse 1 strongly suggests the same collective subject governs Column 2.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The central interpretive question of the entire fragment turns on Column 2 verse 1 — until the people of the Creator arise. Reading 1: the foreign oppressor of Column 1 remains the subject throughout Column 2, and the Creator's support of him in verses 4-11 is the temporary grant of power that YHWH gives to foreign instruments of judgment — as with Nevukhadnetzar in Daniyel 2 and 4 — before the covenant people are finally vindicated. Reading 2: verse 1 of Column 2 marks a complete subject change. The arising of the people of the Creator is the pivotal moment, and from that point all the language of Creator's support, everlasting kingdom, and universal peace belongs to the covenant people and their leader — mirroring Daniyel 7:13-14 and 7:27 precisely, where after the beast kingdoms are destroyed the everlasting dominion is given to the covenant people of the Most High. Reading 2 produces a theologically coherent and structurally elegant document consistent with the entire Qumran apocalyptic tradition. Reading 1 requires that Creator actively empowers an oppressor to trample his own covenant people — a more difficult theological position though not without precedent in the covenant prophetic literature. Both readings are preserved here. The reader is invited to weigh them against the full testimony of the covenant canon.

4Q246 — THE ARAMAIC APOCALYPSE — COMPLETE

Volume Thirty-Two — The Ancient Covenant Record

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

HIS KINGDOM IS AN EVERLASTING KINGDOM

AND ALL HIS PATHS IN TRUTH — HE SHALL JUDGE THE EARTH IN RIGHTEOUSNESS

4Q246 Column 2 — Dead Sea Scrolls Cave 4
